title: Self-Improvement in Language Models: The Sharpening Mechanism

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a unifying perspective on supervision-free self-improvement in language models via a “sharpening” mechanism: using the model as its own verifier to tilt generation toward high self-reward sequences, with the main stylized self-reward instantiated as sequence-level log-likelihood under a base model. The authors develop a statistical “sample-and-evaluate” framework, establish lower bounds governed by a coverage coefficient, and analyze two algorithmic families—SFT-based best-of-N (BoN) amortization and RLHF-style optimization—showing that SFT-Sharpening is minimax optimal under coverage while RLHF-Sharpening can leverage exploration to reduce dependence on coverage via structural complexity (SEC). They present empirical evidence that best-of-N selection based on sequence log-likelihood often improves accuracy and that sharpened models can amortize these gains.
strengths: Introduces a clear and compelling formalization of self-improvement as sequence-level sharpening grounded in the model’s own verifier signal, decoupling “what to optimize” (self-reward) from “how to optimize” (SFT vs RLHF).

Proposes a new sample-and-evaluate framework with information-theoretic lower bounds highlighting the central role of coverage in amortized sharpening, and matches them (up to factors) with SFT-BoN upper bounds.

Identifies a clean objective for RLHF-Sharpening with KL regularization and provides guarantees under a margin condition, then shows how online exploration replaces coverage with the SEC of the model class.
Connects LLM self-improvement to classical sampling-vs-optimization trade-offs and amortization perspectives, offering a principled foundation for future work.

Demonstrates that sequence-level log-likelihood correlates with correctness on a math dataset and that BoN with log-likelihood selection can yield non-trivial accuracy lifts across multiple models and tasks.
Provides both inference-time validation and a training-time amortization sketch, aligning empirical observations with the proposed theory.
The sharpening mechanism and resulting definitions (coverage coefficient, (ε, δ)-sharpened models) are well-motivated and consistently used throughout.
Assumptions are signposted, and the roles of margin, coverage, and SEC are separated and discussed.
Offers one of the first general theoretical treatments of supervision-free LLM self-improvement, with concrete lower/upper bounds that can guide algorithm design.
The coverage vs exploration dichotomy for SFT vs RLHF provides actionable insight for practitioners choosing between amortized BoN and on-policy RL-style methods.
weaknesses: The central self-reward (raw sequence log-likelihood) is stylized and can favor short, generic or safe outputs; theoretical results do not address normalization/regularization necessary in practice to avoid length/pathologies.
Key guarantees for RLHF-Sharpening require a strong margin condition and concentrability bounds; practical validity of these conditions in LLMs is unclear and not empirically probed.
Realizability assumptions (e.g., Π contains the BoN distribution or π*β) may be hard to satisfy with standard fine-tuned LLM parameterizations.

The evaluation appears limited: inference-time BoN results are mixed across tasks/models, and the training-time amortization experiments are only briefly summarized (details relegated to the appendix).
No systematic comparison with stronger self-improvement baselines (e.g., self-consistency, STaR, verifiers-as-judges, CoT decoding with majority voting) using comparable compute budgets.
Lack of ablations on length normalization, alternative self-rewards, or sensitivity to N, β, and data budgets in the amortization pipeline.
The interface between the stylized theoretical setting and practical implementation choices (e.g., tokenization effects, sequence length caps, normalization) is under-specified.
The exact experimental protocol (prompt sets, answer checking, compute, filtering criteria) is not sufficiently detailed in the main text.
Limited empirical comparison to recent self-improvement systems that use judges/verifiers or recursive reasoning; broader alignment literature is cited but not contrasted experimentally.
The paper could better situate its exploration results with other active preference-optimization and pessimistic offline-RL approaches for LLM alignment.
reviews.questions:
1. In experiments, did you use raw sequence log-probability or any normalization/regularization (e.g., length-normalized, per-token average, or penalizations)? How sensitive are BoN and SFT-Sharpening results to these choices?
2. Can you provide empirical evidence for the margin condition (distribution of log-likelihood gaps between top-1 and top-2 sequences) and discuss how it scales with sequence length and decoding temperature?
3. What concrete guidance do your bounds give for practical N (BoN width) and dataset size choices at common coverage levels estimated from modern LLMs? Can you estimate C_cov from your data and show predicted vs observed sample efficiency?
4. Could you include small- to medium-scale RLHF-Sharpening experiments (offline DPO variant and an online exploration variant), to validate assumptions (C_conc, C_loss) and highlight when exploration materially outperforms SFT-Sharpening?
5. How robust is sharpening to distribution shift in μ? If μ is misspecified or broad (e.g., diverse user prompts), does coverage degrade and do your guarantees or empirical results still hold?
6. Can you discuss or quantify risks of degeneracy (e.g., over-short or high-probability but incorrect answers) and how alternative self-rewards (normalized likelihood, judge models) change the theory and practice?
7. How realistic is Assumption 4.1 (Π contains π_N^BoN) for standard fine-tuning of transformer LMs? Any empirical proxy (e.g., NLL fits) or practical recipes (e.g., LoRA ranks) to better satisfy realizability?
reviews.assessment: This paper delivers a compelling, principled account of self-improvement as sharpening, with clean definitions, informative lower bounds, and constructive algorithmic analyses for SFT and RLHF variants. The conceptual clarity—especially the separation of self-reward and optimizer, and the coverage vs exploration trade-off—is valuable and likely to influence subsequent theory and practice. The main weaknesses are on the empirical side: the evaluation centers on inference-time BoN with mixed gains and offers limited training-time amortization evidence and no RLHF-Sharpening experiments. Some theoretical assumptions (margin, realizability, boundedness) are strong and would benefit from empirical calibration. Overall, I find the work original and important; with strengthened experiments (especially for RLHF-Sharpening and ablations on self-reward normalization) and a clearer bridge from stylized theory to practical implementations, it would merit acceptance at ICLR.
